content.definition: Postoperative atrial fibrillation is new AF after surgery, with absent organised P waves and irregularly irregular ventricular response.

content.text: POAF is common after cardiac surgery because atria are inflamed, stretched, catecholamine-exposed and electrolyte-sensitive. It commonly occurs on post-operative days 2-4 but can occur earlier or later.

items: Confirm AF by rhythm strip or 12-lead: absent P waves and irregularly irregular R-R intervals.
Assess stability: MAP, pulse pressure, chest pain, pulmonary oedema, mentation, lactate, urine output and vasoactive dose.
Correct triggers: low K/Mg, hypoxia, hypercarbia, pain, fever, volume overload, hypovolaemia, bleeding, sepsis and catecholamines.
If stable, discuss rate versus rhythm control and anticoagulation in context of bleeding risk.

content.text: AF after valve surgery or LV/RV dysfunction can be haemodynamically severe even when the ventricular rate is only 110-120/min.
Definition and ECG appearance

AF is chaotic atrial electrical activation with no coordinated atrial contraction. There are no consistent P waves and the ventricular response is irregularly irregular unless AV block, pacing or drugs regularise it. QRS is usually narrow unless there is bundle branch block, aberrancy or ventricular pacing.
Why AF is common after cardiac surgery

Surgery causes atrial and pericardial inflammation, atrial stretch, autonomic imbalance, catecholamine exposure, electrolyte disturbance and pericardial irritation. Withdrawal of beta-blockers, hypoxia, pain, sepsis, sleep disruption, volume overload and hypovolaemia also trigger POAF.
Loss of atrial kick

Atrial contraction contributes roughly 15-30 percent of ventricular filling and can be more important in stiff ventricles. In aortic stenosis/AVR, LVH, diastolic dysfunction, mitral valve disease, RV dysfunction and pulmonary hypertension, loss of atrial kick can sharply reduce stroke volume.
Haemodynamic impact by patient type

rows: Fast irregular filling reduces stroke volume and raises oxygen demand.
Low pulse pressure, pulmonary oedema, rising lactate.
Irregular filling worsens RV output and reduces LV preload.
Rising CVP, hypotension, low PA pulsatility.
Atrial contribution and valve filling dynamics are critical.
AF adds irregularity and loss of atrial kick to an already failing pump.

content.text: Rate control versus rhythm control

Rate control slows AV nodal conduction while accepting AF. Rhythm control restores sinus rhythm with drugs or cardioversion. In stable POAF either may be reasonable, but CTICU patients often need rhythm control when AF causes hypotension, low output, ischaemia, RV failure, valve filling problems or inability to wean support.
Beta-blockers, amiodarone and electrolytes

Beta-blockers reduce sympathetic drive and AV nodal conduction but can worsen hypotension, bradycardia, AV block and acute heart failure. Amiodarone is widely used because it can slow rate and facilitate conversion in LV dysfunction, but IV loading can cause hypotension, bradycardia and QT prolongation. Optimise K to 4.0-4.5 mmol/L and Mg to at least 1.0 mmol/L.
Anticoagulation and bleeding risk

POAF increases thromboembolic risk, but early post-cardiac surgery patients have bleeding risk from sternotomy, drains, coagulopathy, pacing wires and possible return to theatre. AF persisting or recurring beyond 48 hours commonly triggers formal anticoagulation assessment, but valve type, LAA management, stroke risk and surgical bleeding risk determine the plan.
Cardioversion indications and setup

Immediate synchronised cardioversion is indicated when AF causes hypotension, shock, ischaemic discomfort, acute heart failure or altered mentation. Apply pads, select synchronised mode, confirm markers on QRS complexes, sedate if feasible, clear the bed and reassess rhythm and perfusion after shock.
POAF often appears just when the patient seems to be improving; do not let timing make it seem harmless.
AF at 110/min can be worse than sinus tachycardia at 130/min if atrial kick is critical.
Correct K and Mg early; low electrolytes are fixable arrhythmia triggers.
Do not anticoagulate a fresh sternotomy patient by formula alone; consider bleeding, drains, pacing wires and surgeon plan.
ECG and monitor setup

For new-onset postoperative atrial fibrillation, the ECG monitor must be treated as a clinical device, not wallpaper. Use a lead that shows atrial activity when P waves matter, print strips during events, check electrode contact, and repeat a 12-lead when rhythm diagnosis, ischaemia or conduction disease is uncertain.
Arterial waveform correlation

The arterial line determines whether electrical activity is producing mechanical output. Rate, rhythm and monitor labels are secondary to MAP, pulse pressure, waveform amplitude, lactate, urine output and clinical perfusion.
Defibrillator and pacing readiness

Unstable AF requires synchronised cardioversion; bradycardia after conversion may require pacing requires early access to pads, synchronised cardioversion, defibrillation or pacing depending on stability. In post-cardiac surgery patients, also check epicardial wires, pacing box battery, output, sensitivity, capture and sensing.

rows: Lead quality, rhythm strip, alarm limits and artefact.
Avoids missed rhythm change or false diagnosis.
Conduction, QRS width, ischaemia and baseline comparison.
Clarifies rhythm and identifies dangerous mimics.
Level, zero, damping, pulse pressure and waveform presence.
Pads, sync markers, battery and energy pathway.
Mode, rate, output, sensitivity and capture threshold.
Essential when rate or AV synchrony fails.
Can occur earlier in ICU or later on ward.
Often clinically significant above 100-110/min; high concern above 120-130/min
Tolerance depends on LV/RV and valve physiology.
Hypotension, shock, ischaemia, acute heart failure or altered mentation
Optimise for AF prevention and treatment.
Persistent/recurrent AF beyond 48 hours often triggers assessment
Balance thromboembolism with surgical bleeding risk.

content.text: Escalate new AF with HR above 120/min, symptoms, new oxygen need, chest pain or electrolyte derangement.
Escalate urgently for AF with hypotension, pulmonary oedema, ischaemia, rising lactate, RV failure or LCOS.
Activate emergency response for peri-arrest AF, severe shock, loss of consciousness or degeneration into VT/VF.

quiz.question: What ECG pattern supports AF?
options: Absent consistent P waves with irregularly irregular R-R intervals.
Regular narrow tachycardia with fixed R-R intervals.
quiz.explanation: AF has no organised P waves and an irregularly irregular ventricular response.

quiz.question: Why is AF poorly tolerated after valve surgery?
options: Loss of atrial kick can markedly reduce ventricular filling.
quiz.explanation: Atrial contraction is often critical in stiff or surgically altered hearts.

quiz.question: When is immediate cardioversion indicated?
options: AF causing hypotension, shock, ischaemia, acute heart failure or altered mentation.
